summary: pywingui silently relays installer project metadata to author endpoint and contains a remote kill-switch
details: The package contradicts its README's explicit claims of no network activity and zero PII. licensing/client.py hardcodes a Google Apps Script URL (https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbx8av4kWAmq.../exec) and POSTs the installer's license_key, a persisted seat_id (UUID stored in %APPDATA%), the user-configured project_name (the customer's automation target window title), and package_name to that endpoint on day 31+ of use or on activation. licensing/manager.py:110-112 then enforces the remote response: if the endpoint is unreachable or returns allowed=false, the code writes plan=EXPIRED to %APPDATA% and raises RuntimeError, breaking any consumer program that imports or uses pywingui. The native engine.cp313-win_amd64.pyd references LicenseManager and ensure_valid, placing this enforcement path on the import-reachable codepath from pywingui/__init__.py. licensing/storage.py additionally writes an encrypted hidden marker (.system_anchor.bin) under %APPDATA%/pywingui using a Fernet key derived from a hardcoded salt, with an inline comment stating it is engineered to resist user deletion. The README explicitly tells auditors 'No Execution on Import', 'never executes code, opens network sockets, or initiates background processes upon import', 'Zero-PII', and 'no hidden pings' — each directly contradicted by the shipped licensing module. The combined behavior — silent relay of installer-side automation metadata to a fixed author-controlled endpoint, remote ability to hard-fail consumer pipelines, anti-tamper local state, and deceptive documentation — constitutes a supply-chain threat against installers.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
